one_line_takeaway: Greedy-decoding evaluations falsely certify unlearning and alignment; sampling recovers the 'forgotten' or toxic content, and this paper gives statistically-guaranteed probabilistic leakage metrics plus an entropy-optimization defense that closes the gap.
key_finding: Deterministic greedy evaluations systematically underestimate leakage: all state-of-the-art unlearning methods and aligned models leak the supposedly-removed or toxic information once you sample from the distribution. On the Harry Potter Q&A, greedy decoding indicates successful unlearning, yet the probabilistic binary bound shows that for 38% of questions the upper bound on expected leakage exceeds 10%. On TOFU (90/10), models with near-identical greedy ROUGE-L still differ substantially in their sampled distributions. The proposed entropy-optimization + adaptive-temperature method (built on NPO) drives sampled leakage variance toward zero (Std. Dev. 0.00 and ED score 0.20 vs NPO's 0.34) while preserving output diversity on retain data and leaving model utility essentially unchanged.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a formal probabilistic evaluation framework for LLMs that assesses a model's entire output distribution rather than a single greedy point estimate. Specifically it introduces (1) four probabilistic leakage metrics with high-probability (1-alpha) guarantees derived from distribution-free concentration bounds via Monte-Carlo sampling: a binary leakage bound Mbin (Clopper-Pearson), a general CDF leakage bound Mgen (Dvoretzky-Kiefer-Wolfowitz), an expectation bound Mmu (Anderson 1969), and a standard-deviation bound Msigma; (2) a lightweight development-time Expectation-Deviation (ED) score S_ED = S_mean + rho * S_sd (rho=2 in experiments); and (3) a new unlearning method combining an entropy-optimization loss (minimize output-distribution entropy on the forget set, slightly maximize it on the retain set via weights lambda_f>0, lambda_r<0, applied on top of an existing loss such as NPO) with adaptive temperature scaling (set temperature to 0 when average token confidence exceeds a threshold cT). The framework is application-independent and demonstrated on both unlearning and safety alignment. NOTE: machine-unlearning algorithms it builds on/compares against (GA, GD, RMU, NPO, DPO, TOFU/Harry-Potter benchmarks) are cited prior work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: An adversary (or an auditor checking compliance) who can sample from a deployed LLM rather than only greedy-decode it. The paper shows that unlearning and safety-alignment methods evaluated with deterministic greedy decoding appear successful, yet the removed/forgotten information (e.g., private data, copyrighted content, Harry Potter facts) or toxic content is still embedded in the broader output distribution and is recovered simply by multinomial/top-p sampling. Even a small fraction of leaking samples (e.g., a revealed SSN, password, or harmful instruction) is treated as a real risk. The framework is defensive: it quantifies residual leakage risk with statistical guarantees and the entropy-optimization method reduces it.
limitations: The framework cannot assess the entire output distribution holistically for every possible input; due to compute it only analyzes distributions for fixed inputs, not, e.g., inputs within an edit-distance neighborhood (relevant to adversarial alignment). Guarantees are per-query bounds that depend on the sample size n (n=1024 used) and on the fidelity of the leakage oracle h (ROUGE-L / keyword match / toxicity classifier), so oracle error propagates into the metric. Unlearning experiments use only Phi-1.5 (plus the single Harry-Potter Llama-2 model); the strongest new results (entropy optimization) are demonstrated on top of NPO specifically.

datasets_used: TOFU (200 fictitious author profiles; 90/10, 95/5, 99/1 retain/forget splits; plus Real Authors and World Facts utility sets)
Harry Potter Q&A dataset (question + relevant-keyword pairs) evaluated on the Llama-2-Who-is-Harry-Potter model
JailbreakBench (JBB) 100 harmful behaviors dataset (toxicity scored by the Harmbench toxicity classifier)
quantitative_results: Harry Potter Q&A: under greedy decoding unlearning looks successful, but the probabilistic binary bound shows for 38% of questions the upper bound on expected leakage > 10%
TOFU 90/10 deterministic ROUGE-L (lower=better): RMU 0.70, GD 0.33, GA 0.32, NPO 0.22, Ours 0.20
TOFU 90/10 probabilistic ED score: RMU 0.81, GD 0.42, GA 0.41, NPO 0.34, Ours 0.20
TOFU 90/10 sampled Mean leakage: RMU 0.60, GD 0.32, GA 0.31, NPO 0.21, Ours 0.20
TOFU 90/10 sampled Std. Dev.: RMU 0.10, GD 0.05, GA 0.05, NPO 0.06, Ours 0.00
Entropy-regularization utility (TOFU 99 split, lambda_f=1): lambda_r=0 -> 0.496, -0.125 -> 0.509, -0.250 -> 0.504, -0.500 -> 0.490 (no consistent negative effect on utility)

Alignment (JBB): across Phi-1.5, Vicuna-7b-1.5, Mistral-7b-instruct-v0.3, expected toxicity under sampling consistently exceeds greedy toxicity; Mbin shows all models are not robust under sampling despite greedy appearing robust
Evaluation config: n=1024 samples per query, alpha=0.01, top-p=0.9, adaptive-temperature threshold cT=0.9, NPO beta=0.05
baselines_compared: NPO (Negative Preference Optimization) - also the base for the authors' method
Ours = NPO + entropy optimization + adaptive temperature scaling
Deterministic greedy-decoding evaluation (the point-estimate baseline the paper argues against)
